title: Olearia viscosa
intro: Olearia viscosa, also known as sticky tree daisy or sticky leaf bush, is a shrubby perennial plant native to eastern Australia, characterized by its small white flowers and sticky leaves. Traditionally, the plant has been used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including respiratory issues and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Olearia viscosa include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, Olearia viscosa is commonly prepared as a tincture, tea, or infused oil, and is used to relieve coughs, colds, and sore throats, as well as to soothe skin conditions such as eczema and acne.
taxonomy: Olearia viscosa is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is one of the largest and most widespread in the plant kingdom. Olearia viscosa is further classified in the genus Olearia, which is native to Australia and New Zealand. The genus Olearia is a member of the tribe Astereae and contains around 150 species of shrubs and small trees. Olearia viscosa is also known as sticky Daisy bush in some regions due to its sticky or viscid leaves.
uses: Olearia viscosa has been traditionally used by the Indigenous Australians for its medicinal properties, although specific uses are limited.
